seo.title: Next.js Analytics — OpenPanel SDK for App Router & Pages Router
seo.description: Add analytics to your Next.js app in minutes. OpenPanel's Next.js SDK supports App Router, Pages Router, server-side events, and automatic route change tracking. Open source, cookieless, from $2.50/month.

hero.heading: Analytics Built for Next.js
hero.subheading: Most analytics tools treat Next.js like a static site. OpenPanel was built for how Next.js actually works — App Router, server components, API routes, and route changes all tracked correctly. Install in 5 minutes, get events, funnels, and user profiles that work with SSR.

problem.title: Why standard analytics breaks in Next.js
problem.intro: Next.js is not a traditional website. Most analytics tools were built before App Router existed and show it.

items.title: GA4 breaks with App Router route changes
items.description: Google Analytics was designed for traditional page loads. In Next.js App Router, client-side navigation doesn't trigger GA4's page view events unless you add custom workarounds. Your analytics misses a significant portion of page views.

items.title: Cookie consent is painful in Next.js
items.description: Implementing a GDPR-compliant cookie consent flow in a Next.js app requires next/script, consent state management, and conditional script loading. It's a non-trivial engineering task just to run analytics.

items.title: No server-side tracking
items.description: Most analytics SDKs are client-only. Server-side events — API route completions, background jobs, server actions — are invisible. You're missing half the picture.

items.title: Analytics libraries bloat your bundle
items.description: Many analytics SDKs add 20–50 KB to your JavaScript bundle. In a performance-conscious Next.js app, that overhead matters for Core Web Vitals.

features.title: Analytics that works the way Next.js works
features.intro: OpenPanel's Next.js SDK was designed for modern Next.js patterns — not adapted from a legacy client-only library.

items.title: Next.js SDK with App Router support
items.description: Install @openpanel/nextjs, add the <OpenPanelComponent> to your root layout, and automatic route change tracking works immediately across App Router and Pages Router.

items.title: Automatic route change tracking
items.description: Every router.push(), <Link> navigation, and back/forward browser action is captured as a page view. No custom useEffect or router event listeners needed.

items.title: Server-side event tracking
items.description: Import openPanel in any server component, API route, or server action to track events server-side. Track form submissions, payment completions, and background jobs without any client involvement.

items.title: Cookieless by default
items.description: The OpenPanel SDK uses no cookies. No consent management library, no conditional script loading, no GDPR consent modal needed — just install and track.

items.title: Identify users across sessions
items.description: Call op.identify({ profileId, name, email }) after authentication to tie anonymous events to known users. Works with any auth solution including NextAuth.js, Clerk, and custom implementations.

items.title: TypeScript-first SDK
items.description: Full TypeScript types for all methods. Autocomplete for event names and properties. Zero runtime errors from mistyped event calls.

items.title: 2.3 KB gzipped client bundle
items.description: The smallest full-featured analytics SDK for Next.js. No impact on Lighthouse scores or Core Web Vitals.

benefits.title: Why Next.js developers choose OpenPanel
benefits.intro: OpenPanel fits naturally into a modern Next.js codebase — not as an afterthought.

items.title: Works exactly how Next.js works
items.description: Built for App Router, server components, and modern Next.js patterns. Not bolted onto a client-only library.

items.title: No consent infrastructure needed
items.description: Cookieless tracking means no consent modal, no next/script loading gymnastics, no conditional initialization. Install once, works everywhere.

items.title: Track server and client events in the same dashboard
items.description: Server actions, API endpoints, and client interactions all show up together. Full picture of what your app is doing.

items.title: Open source and self-hostable
items.description: Run OpenPanel on your own infrastructure. Your Next.js app's analytics data never leaves your servers.

items.title: From $2.50/month
items.description: No enterprise contract, no per-seat fees. Pay for events, get all features. Start free with 30-day trial.

faqs.title: Frequently asked questions
faqs.intro: Common questions from Next.js developers evaluating OpenPanel.

items.question: How do I install OpenPanel in a Next.js app?
items.answer: Install @openpanel/nextjs, add <OpenPanelComponent clientId="..." /> to your root layout.tsx, and you're tracking page views. For custom events, import and call op.track('event_name', { properties }) anywhere. See the full step-by-step guide at /guides/nextjs-analytics.

items.question: Does OpenPanel support Next.js App Router?
items.answer: Yes. The <OpenPanelComponent> handles App Router route changes automatically using the usePathname hook internally. No custom setup is needed for client-side navigation tracking.

items.question: Can I track events in Server Components and API Routes?
items.answer: Yes. Import { openPanel } from @openpanel/nextjs/server and call openPanel.track() in any server context including Server Components, Route Handlers, and Server Actions.

items.question: Does OpenPanel work with NextAuth.js or Clerk?
items.answer: Yes. Call op.identify({ profileId: session.user.id, ... }) after authentication to link events to user identities. Works with any auth solution that gives you a user ID.

items.question: Does OpenPanel add significant bundle size to my Next.js app?
items.answer: No. The client-side SDK is 2.3 KB gzipped. For reference, GA4 adds 50+ KB. OpenPanel has negligible impact on your bundle size or Core Web Vitals.

items.question: Can I self-host OpenPanel for my Next.js app?
items.answer: Yes. OpenPanel is fully open source and can be self-hosted with Docker. Your Next.js app sends events to your own server. The self-hosted version has no event limits and is free to run.
